nativeRange.habitat: Tropical and subtropical intertidal to shallow subtidal hard-bottom and reef environments; prefers high-flow areas

avoidWith: aggressive herbivorous fish that may overgraze

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 4152x4152. Dictyota cyanoloma in situ showing the signature iridescent blue-green ribbon tips alongside gold/brown dichotomously forked blades — hits every diagnostic feature; sharp, vibrant hero shot.
Bernard Picton; 1024x681. Dictyota dichotoma in situ, textbook golden-brown flat ribbon with clear repeated dichotomous forking; sharp, whole thallus framed.
Erasmo Macaya Horta; 800x600. Dictyota kunthii, vibrant golden clump of ribbon blades on reef in situ; small corner watermark and modest resolution the only caveats.
Jesse Rorabaugh; 971x1024. Dictyota flabellata, complete fresh golden-brown specimen showing dichotomous forking; clinical tray background but sharp and true to colour.
Thomas Irvine; 905x1024. Dictyota pulchella, whole gold specimen with slender forked branches in a clear dish; sharp, single subject, background clinical.
Tony Rebelo; 1024x683. Dictyota naevosa in situ, olive-green strap-like forked blades with reproductive sori spots; slight water-surface glare but adds an in-habitat form.

summary: Brown Ribbon is a fast-growing saltwater macroalga whose flat, ribbon-like branches fork repeatedly and range in color from yellow and gold to brown, with some specimens developing iridescent blue-green tips under good conditions. Available from specialty online retailers, it is considered a potential nuisance if growth goes unchecked in a reef tank.
careNotes:
Brown Ribbon belongs to the genus Dictyota (family Dictyotaceae, order Dictyotales, class Phaeophyceae — the brown algae). The genus encompasses approximately 237 species, all sharing the characteristic flat, ribbon-like body (thallus) that branches by splitting repeatedly in two — a pattern called dichotomous forking, which gives rise to the trade names Brown Forked, Iridescent Forked, and Stubby Forked. Coloration tends toward yellow, gold, and brown, and specimens have been reported to display striking blue-green iridescent tips under optimal conditions.

Three morphological forms are commonly encountered in the hobby: a taller variety marketed as Brown Forked Algae, a smaller clumping form with more pronounced iridescence sold as Iridescent Dictyota, and a compact variety known as Stubby Forked. All are genus-level identifications only — no species epithet has been confirmed for the aquarium trade specimens. The genus is widely distributed across tropical and subtropical marine environments worldwide, with particular dominance on Caribbean reef systems; Dictyota has been documented comprising roughly 70% of benthic biomass in the Florida Keys reef tract.

**Invasive/nuisance caveat:** Dictyota is widely considered a nuisance alga in reef aquariums. Under favorable conditions — moderate lighting, elevated nutrients, and turbulent flow — it tends to grow rapidly and can overgrow corals or other invertebrates. The genus produces terpenoids (chemical compounds) that inhibit grazing by sea urchins, herbivorous amphipods, and many fish, making biological control less effective than with other algae. Those intentionally cultivating it as an ornamental display specimen are advised to monitor growth closely and trim regularly. Adding it to an established reef should be done with caution.

This alga appreciates turbulent water flow, standard reef water parameters, and moderate lighting. It generally does not require supplemental fertilization in an established reef system. Propagation is readily achieved through fragmentation — detached pieces attach to rockwork and resume growth. As a brown alga, this species will MELT at the base if buried in sand. Attach frags to rockwork using superglue, zip-tie or fishing line, or by wedging into crevices instead. (Sand-bed placement is generally appropriate only for genera such as Caulerpa, Halimeda, Penicillus, Rhipocephalus, and Udotea, plus some calcified Dasycladales.)
breedingNotes: Propagation in aquariums is typically achieved through fragmentation — broken or trimmed pieces readily attach to rockwork and resume growth. Wild Dictyota populations reproduce sexually through an alternation of generations between gametophyte and sporophyte phases, but this reproductive cycle has not been reported as a practical propagation method in the hobby.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source for this species. Three Dictyota sp. SKUs consolidated into this entry.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Iridescent Dictyota
additional.notes: Second Mosaic SKU for this genus; describes iridescent blue coloration under optimal conditions and preference for turbulent flow.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Stubby Forked
additional.notes: Third Mosaic SKU for this genus; confirms turbulent flow preference and brownish-yellow coloration description.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Dictyota
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy: family Dictyotaceae, order Dictyotales, class Phaeophyceae. Describes flat ribbon-like dichotomously branched thalli; tropical/subtropical distribution; ~237 species in genus.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Dictyotales
additional.notes: Confirms Dictyotaceae family. Notes Dictyota dominates ~70% of benthic biomass in Florida Keys reef tract and produces grazer-deterring terpenoids — supports nuisance/invasive caveat.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
